ontology.foodon_label: hazelnut and chocolate spread food product
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for hazelnut chocolate spread; SNOMED CT coverage for processed condiment spreads is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of hazelnut and chocolate-flavoured spread is approximately 15 g (one tablespoon), commonly spread on toast, bread, or crackers as a breakfast or snack accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Hazelnut and Chocolate-Flavoured Spread — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for hazelnut and chocolate-flavoured spread (cr_id F10010006). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chocolate Hazelnut Spread — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 40 (low) is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database data for chocolate hazelnut spread products (Nutella and analogous products). The high fat and sugar content moderates the glycaemic response relative to pure sugar. Glycemic load per 15 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (40 × 8.67) / 100 ≈ 3.5. Input record GI confidence was 0.346, reflecting analogous-estimate basis; this is retained and documented here.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (57.2 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention despite low sodium (33 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium (430 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium, the extreme sugar load (57.2 g/100 g) independently elevates blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation; individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this spread to trace amounts.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 57.2 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; minimal protein (6.8 g) and moderate fibre (3.7 g) are insufficient to modulate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 57.2 g sugar per 100 g, this spread is contraindicated for individuals with type 2 diabetes; even a 15 g serving delivers approximately 8.6 g of sugar, and substitution with natural nut butters (almond, peanut) is strongly recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 10.8 g/100 g from palm oil (a pro-atherogenic fat), combined with ultra-high sugar (57.2 g/100 g) and ultra-processed formulation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of palm-oil-derived saturated fat (~10.8 g/100 g) and very high sugar (57.2 g/100 g) in an ultra-processed product raises LDL cholesterol and triglyceride risk; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and limit serving size to no more than 5 g on an occasional basis.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Palm oil as primary fat source is pro-inflammatory and explicitly excluded from the MUFA/PUFA nut-oil carve-out; refined sugar at 57.2 g/100 g further promotes inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Palm oil (primary fat, ~10.8 g saturated fat/100 g) is pro-inflammatory and not offset by the hazelnut content; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this spread and choose anti-inflammatory alternatives such as extra-virgin olive oil or pure nut butters.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hazelnut and chocolate-flavoured spread is a smooth, sweet, commercially produced condiment made primarily from sugar, palm oil, roasted hazelnuts, and cocoa powder, with added skimmed milk powder and emulsifiers. It is an ultra-processed product with a dense, creamy texture and a rich chocolate-hazelnut flavour profile. Globally popularised by brands such as Nutella, it is widely consumed as a bread spread at breakfast or as a snack topping on toast, crackers, crepes, and waffles. Per 100 g, it delivers 518 kcal, 29.6 g fat (of which approximately 10.8 g is saturated fat from palm oil), 57.8 g carbohydrate (57.2 g sugar), 6.8 g protein, and 3.7 g dietary fibre. Sodium is low at 33 mg/100 g, while potassium (430 mg) and calcium (130 mg) are notable positives. Clinically, the product is high-concern for type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its extreme sugar load and palm-oil-derived saturated fat. The glycaemic index is approximately 40 (low), moderated by fat content, but the glycaemic load per 15 g serving is still approximately 3.5 g. It is vegetarian and generally halal-certified but contains milk and tree nut allergens. Regular or liberal use is not recommended for individuals managing blood glucose, blood pressure, or cardiovascular risk.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in hazelnut spread?
Is chocolate hazelnut spread good for high blood pressure?
What are the calories in Nutella per tablespoon?
Does hazelnut chocolate spread contain palm oil?
What is the GI of chocolate hazelnut spread?
Is Nutella suitable for heart patients?
What is a healthier alternative to hazelnut chocolate spread?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10010006) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a well-characterised commercially produced product with consistent published nutrient profiles. GI is based on analogous-estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for chocolate hazelnut spread products, reducing GI confidence to medium; however, overall record confidence is maintained at high given the strong HPB nutrient anchor.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel beyond potassium, calcium, and iron, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this product as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central (Nutella, NDB 19906) provides partial fatty acid data (monounsaturated ~12.5 g, polyunsaturated ~3.2 g per 100 g) but these values are for the branded Nutella formulation and may not be identical to the HPB-sourced generic record; they have not been substituted to preserve source integrity. 2. Saturated fat of approximately 10.8 g/100 g is estimated from the known palm oil composition of hazelnut chocolate spreads and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous products; this is an estimate and not directly reported in the HPB record. 3. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for chocolate hazelnut spread; input record GI confidence was 0.346. 4. The input record listed 'high_fibre' as a dietary tag; this is not retained as 3.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g does not meet the Singapore HPB high-fibre threshold of ≥6 g/100 g for solid foods. 5. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. The product is of Western/international origin and is consumed globally including in Singapore.

Populated gi_source with University of Sydney GI Database analogous-estimate basis and calculated GL per 15 g serving.

Expanded health_context to include all three required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with specific clinical guidance; added rheumatoid_arthritis as additional condition.

Set ontology with defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes; SNOMED CT null with gap note.
